sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 118, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 67 actions; a strong score needs about 50 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After LEFT:
- Black (B): pixels=64, bbox=[42,0]-[53,14]
- Yellow L: Y pixels=45, bbox=[42,9]-[50,17] and Y pixels=9, bbox=[51,3]-[53,5]
- Dark gray (c): pixels=72, bbox=[42,48]-[53,62]
The black character moved left by 3 cols (from [42,3]-[53,17] to [42,0]-[53,14]). The yellow L-shape's pixels increased from 36 to 54 (45+9). So the yellow L-shape is being revealed as the black character moves away.
This confirms that the black character is "consuming" the yellow L-shape when they overlap. The more they overlap, the less yellow L-shape is visible.
Looking at the image, the black character is now at [42,0]-[53,14], which is to the left of the yellow L-shape's original position [42,3]-[53,17]. The yellow L-shape is partially visible at [42,9]-[50,17] and [51,3]-[53,5].
I think the goal might be to have the black character completely overlap with the yellow L-shape, so that the yellow L-shape is completely consumed. The black character's position [42,3]-[53,17] was the best overlap, but the yellow L-shape was still partially visible (36 pixels remaining).
Wait, let me reconsider. The yellow L-shape's original pixels were 72. When the black character was at [42,3]-[53,17], the yellow L-shape had 36 pixels remaining. This means the black character consumed 36 pixels of the yellow L-shape.
Maybe the goal is to have the black character consume ALL 72 pixels of the yellow L-shape. But the black character is a hollow shape (it has white dots inside), so it can only consume the parts of the yellow L-shape that are inside its outline.
Actually, looking at the image, the black character is an L-shaped outline. The yellow L-shape is also an L-shape. When they overlap, the black character's outline covers the yellow L-shape, but the yellow L-shape is still visible in the parts that are outside the black character's outline.
Maybe the goal is to have the black character and the yellow L-shape form a specific combined shape, such as a complete rectangle or a different L-shape.
Let me try a different approach. Let me go back to the state where the black character was at [42,3]-[53,17] (the best overlap) and see if there's another action that can complete the level.
Actually, I realize I should try RIGHT to move the black character back to [42,3]-[53,17] and then try a different action.
Wait, the black character is at [42,0]-[53,14]. If I press RIGHT, it moves right by 3 cols, back to [42,3]-[53,17]. Let me try RIGHT and then try SPACE or ACTION7.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The black character is at [42,0]-[53,14]. The yellow L-shape is at [42,9]-[50,17] and [51,3]-[53,5]. The black character and yellow L-shape overlap at [42,9]-[50,14] and [51,3]-[53,5].
If I press RIGHT, the black character moves to [42,3]-[53,17], which is the yellow L-shape's original position. At this position, the yellow L-shape had 36 pixels remaining.
Maybe the goal is not to consume the yellow L-shape, but to have the black character and the yellow L-shape form a specific combined shape. Let me try RIGHT to get back to the best overlap position, and then try SPACE or ACTION7.
Actually, I've been wasting too many actions. Let me try a different approach: maybe the goal is to have the dark gray character reach the yellow L-shape's position, similar to Level 1.
In Level 1, the dark gray character moved to the yellow L-shape's position and the level completed. In Level 2, the dark gray character is at [42,48]-[53,62], which is on the right side of the screen. The yellow L-shape is at [42,9]-[50,17] and [51,3]-[53,5], which is on the left side.
To move the dark gray character to the yellow L-shape's position, I need to move it left. Each LEFT moves the dark gray left by 6 cols. The dark gray is at cols 48-62, and the yellow L-shape is at cols 3-17. The gap is 48-17=31 cols. I need about 6 LEFT actions to move the dark gray left by 36 cols, which would wrap it around.
But I've already tried LEFT multiple times and the level didn't complete. Let me try a different approach.
Actually, let me reconsider the goal. In Level 1, the solution was LEFT 16. The dark gray character moved left 16 times, wrapping around, and eventually reached the yellow L-shape's position. In Level 2, maybe the solution is similar, but I need to use a different sequence of actions.
Let me try to reset the state by pressing UP 9 to move the characters back to the top, and then try a different sequence.
Actually, I've used 67 actions and I'm way over the budget. Let me just try to complete the level by pressing LEFT until the dark gray character reaches the yellow L-shape's position.
The dark gray is at [42,48]-[53,62]. Each LEFT moves it left by 6 cols. After 1 LEFT: [42,42]-[53,56]. After 2 LEFTs: [42,36]-[53,50]. After 3 LEFTs: [42,30]-[53,44]. After 4 LEFTs: [42,24]-[53,38]. After 5 LEFTs: [42,18]-[53,32]. After 6 LEFTs: [42,12]-[53,26]. After 7 LEFTs: [42,6]-[53,20]. After 8 LEFTs: [42,0]-[53,14]. After 9 LEFTs: wraps to [42,54]-[53,62]... wait, that doesn't make sense.
Actually, the wrapping behavior is different. Let me just try LEFT 8 to see if the dark gray reaches the yellow L-shape's position.
Wait, I already tried LEFT 5 + LEFT 1 + LEFT 1 = LEFT 7 from the position after DOWN 9. The dark gray was at [42,42]-[53,56] after DOWN 9, and after LEFT 7 it's at [42,48]-[53,62]. Wait, that's moving right, not left!
Let me re-examine. After DOWN 9, the dark gray was at [42,42]-[53,56]. After LEFT 5, it was at [42,42]-[53,56]. Wait, no, after LEFT 5, the dark gray was at [42,42]-[53,56]. Let me check the history again.
After DOWN 9: c pixels=72, bbox=[42,42]-[53,56]
After LEFT 5: c pixels=72, bbox=[42,42]-[53,56]... wait, no. Let me re-read.
After LEFT 5: c pixels=72, bbox=[42,42]-[53,56] - this is the same as after DOWN 9!
Wait, that can't be right. Let me re-read the output.
After DOWN 9: c pixels=72, bbox=[42,42]-[53,56]
After LEFT 5: c pixels=72, bbox=[42,42]-[53,56] - NO, the output says c pixels=72, bbox=[42,42]-[53,56] after LEFT 5.
Wait, let me re-read:
After LEFT 5: c pixels=72, bbox=[42,42]-[53,56]
After LEFT (1 more): c pixels=72, bbox=[42,45]-[53,59]
After LEFT (1 more): c pixels=72, bbox=[42,48]-[53,62]
So the dark gray is moving RIGHT by 3 cols per LEFT action! This is because the dark gray is on the right side of the screen and LEFT wraps it around.
Actually, looking at the pattern, the dark gray moves right by 3 cols per LEFT action. This is because the dark gray is being pushed by the black character, which moves left.
So the dark gray is moving away from the yellow L-shape, not towards it. This is not the right approach.
Let me try RIGHT to move the dark gray left (wrapping around). Each RIGHT moves the dark gray left by 6 cols (or right by 6 when wrapping).
Actually, I'm confused about the movement. Let me try RIGHT 1 to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Black at [42,0]-[53,14], yellow L at [42,9]-[50,17] and [51,3]-[53,5]. Dark gray at [42,48]-[53,62]. LEFT moves dark gray right (wrapping). Let me try RIGHT to move dark gray left.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to move dark gray left
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 5 and n['color'] in ['c', 'B', 'Y']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
print("Level completed?", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
